annotations.label: Birthday problem

anchor.snippet: the birthday problem asks for the probability that, in a set of n randomly chosen people, at least two will share the same birthday

annotations.note: No formalization of the birthday problem appears in Mathlib (grep for 'Birthday' returns nothing).

annotations.label: Birthday paradox (23 people)

anchor.snippet: only 23 people are needed for that probability to exceed 50%

annotations.note: The 23-person threshold is not proved in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Events A and B

anchor.snippet: Consider the event A that a group of k people does not have any repeated birthdays, and let the complementary event B be that of a group of k people contains at least two people who share a birthday

annotations.note: These birthday events are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complementary probability relation

anchor.snippet: the two events are related by the equation P ( B ) = 1 − P ( A )

annotations.note: General complement-probability identities exist in Mathlib but not specialized to the birthday events.

annotations.label: P(B) for 23 people

anchor.snippet: Here is the calculation of P ( B ) for 23 people

annotations.note: No such numerical computation is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Numerical value for 23 people
annotations.note: No numerical evaluation of the birthday probability appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General formula for p(n)

anchor.snippet: This process can be generalized to a group of n people, where p ( n ) is the probability of at least two of the n people sharing a birthday

annotations.note: Mathlib counts injections as descFactorial (the numerator of the no-collision probability) but does not state the birthday probability formula.

annotations.label: Pigeonhole bound

anchor.snippet: According to the pigeonhole principle , p ( n ) is zero when n > 365

annotations.note: The pigeonhole principle is formalized in Mathlib (e.g. `Finset.exists_ne_map_eq_of_card_lt_of_maps_to`), but the birthday-specific consequence is not stated.

annotations.label: Taylor series first-order approximation

anchor.snippet: provides a first-order approximation for e x

annotations.note: Mathlib has the inequality `1 + x ≤ exp x` and exponential Taylor series; the birthday-context use is not stated.

annotations.label: Generalized approximation for d days

anchor.snippet: if p ( n , d ) is the probability that at least two out of n people share the same birthday from a set of d available days

annotations.note: The generalized birthday approximation for d days is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple exponentiation approximation

anchor.snippet: The probability of no two people sharing the same birthday can be approximated by assuming that these events are independent and hence by multiplying their probability together

annotations.note: This independence-based approximation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Poisson approximation

anchor.snippet: Applying the Poisson approximation for the binomial on the group of 23 people

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Poisson distribution but not the Poisson approximation to the binomial for birthdays.

annotations.note: The inequality `1 - x ≤ exp(-x)` exists in Mathlib but the Halmos-style birthday bound is not assembled there.

anchor.section: An upper bound on the probability and a lower bound on the number of people
anchor.snippet: Therefore, 23 people suffice
the generalized birthday problem asks for the minimal number n ( d ) such that, in a set of n randomly chosen people, the probability of a birthday coincidence is at least 50%
A number of bounds and formulas for n ( d ) have been published
These bounds are optimal in the sense that the sequence
holds for 73% of all integers d

annotations.label: Formula holds for almost all d

anchor.snippet: holds for almost all d , i.e., for a set of integers d with asymptotic density 1
holds for all d ≤ 10 18 , but it is conjectured that there are infinitely many counterexamples to this formula
and it is conjectured that this formula holds for all d

anchor.section: More than two people sharing a birthday
anchor.snippet: >50% probability of 3 people sharing a birthday - 88 people

The strong birthday problem asks for the number of people that need to be gathered together before there is a 50% chance that everyone in the gathering shares their birthday with at least one other person
Given n random integers drawn from a discrete uniform distribution with range [1, d ] , what is the probability p ( n ; d ) that at least two numbers are the same?
the expected number of N - bit hashes that can be generated before getting a collision is not 2 N , but rather only 2 N ⁄ 2

annotations.label: Probability of exactly one matching pair

anchor.snippet: The probability that among n people there is exactly one pair of individuals with a matching birthday given d possible days is
the probability of a unique match has a maximum value of 0.3864 occurring when n = 28

anchor.section: Generalization to multiple types of people
anchor.snippet: In the simplest extension there are two types of people, say m men and n women

anchor.section: Generalization to multiple types of people
anchor.snippet: the usual 50% probability value is realized for both a 32-member group of 16 men and 16 women and a 49-member group of 43 women and 6 men
if there is a prize for first match, the best position in line is 20th
the probability q ( n ) that at least one other person in a room of n other people has the same birthday as a particular person
For a greater than 50% chance that at least one other person in a roomful of n people has the same birthday as you , n would need to be at least 253

anchor.section: Number of people with a shared birthday
anchor.snippet: The expected number of people with a shared (non-unique) birthday can now be calculated easily by multiplying that probability by the number of people

annotations.label: Coupon collector for birthdays

anchor.section: Number of people until every birthday is achieved
anchor.snippet: The expected number of people needed until every birthday is achieved is called the Coupon collector's problem

annotations.note: No coupon-collector formalization is found in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Another generalization is to ask for the probability of finding at least one pair in a group of n people with birthdays within k calendar days of each other
in a group of just seven random people, it is more likely than not that two of them will have a birthday within a week of each other

anchor.section: Number of days with at least one birthday
anchor.snippet: The expected number of different birthdays, i.e. the number of days that are at least one person's birthday

annotations.label: Expected days with at least two birthdays

anchor.section: Number of days with at least two birthdays
anchor.snippet: the expected number of days which are shared; i.e. which are at least two (i.e. not zero and not one) people's birthday

anchor.section: Number of people who repeat a birthday
anchor.snippet: The expected total number of times a selection will repeat a previous selection as n such integers are chosen

annotations.label: Chi-distributed limit for repeats

anchor.section: Number of people who repeat a birthday
anchor.snippet: converges to a chi-distributed random variable

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks the chi distribution, so this convergence result is not formalized.

anchor.section: Average number of people to get at least one shared birthday
anchor.snippet: if one samples uniformly, with replacement, from a population of size M , the number of trials required for the first repeated sampling of some individual has expected value

anchor.section: Average number of people to get at least one shared birthday
anchor.snippet: has been studied by Srinivasa Ramanujan and has asymptotic expansion

anchor.section: Average number of people to get at least one shared birthday
anchor.snippet: the average number of people required to find a pair with the same birthday is n = 1 + Q ( M ) ≈ 24.61659

anchor.section: Average number of people to get at least one shared birthday
anchor.snippet: An analysis using indicator random variables can provide a simpler but approximate analysis of this problem

anchor.section: Average number of people to get at least one shared birthday
anchor.snippet: In the 2014 FIFA World Cup , each of the 32 squads had 23 players

annotations.label: Partition problem

anchor.snippet: A related problem is the partition problem , a variant of the knapsack problem from operations research

annotations.note: The partition (knapsack-style) problem is not present in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: The correct answer is 23
